original_abstract: Although isomerism is a typical and significant phenomenon in organic chemistry, it is rarely found in covalent organic framework (COF) materials. Herein, for the first time, we report a controllable synthesis of topological isomers in three-dimensional COFs via a distinctive tetrahedral building unit under different solvents. Based on this strategy, both isomers with a dia or qtz net (termed JUC-620 and JUC-621) have been obtained, and their structures are determined by combining powder X-ray diffraction and transmission electron microscopy. Remarkably, these architectures show a distinct difference in their porous features; for example, JUC-621 with a qtz net exhibits permanent mesopores (up to ∼23 Å) and high surface area (∼2060 m2 g-1), which far surpasses those of JUC-620 with a dia net (pore size of ∼12 Å and surface area of 980 m2 g-1). Furthermore, mesoporous JUC-621 can remove dye molecules efficiently and achieves excellent iodine adsorption (up to 6.7 g g-1), which is 2.3 times that of microporous JUC-620 (∼2.9 g g-1). This work thus provides a new way for constructing COF isomers and promotes structural diversity and promising applications of COF materials.
humanized_abstract: Isomerism is a common phenomenon in organic chemistry but is not often seen in the covalent organic frameworks (COFs). Herein, we demonstrated the first example of controllable synthesis of COF topological isomers by using a unique tetrahedral OB unit precursor and diverse solvents, affording two 3D COFs with dia and qtz nets as isomers, denoted as JUC-620 and JUC-621. Their precise structures are confirmed by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) studies. Notably, the distinct porous structures are observed in these two isomers, in which the qtz-net COF JUC-621 shows permanent mesopores up to 23 Å in diameter and a specific surface area as high as 2060 m2 g-1, which is much larger than those of the microporous dia-net COF JUC-620 (pore size around 12 Å and specific surface area of 980 m2 g-1). Furthermore, the high surface area and mesoporous structure of JUC-621 endow it with good applicability for the removal of dye solutions and iodine adsorption with a capacity of up to 6.7 g g-1 (2.3 times that of JUC-620, 2.9 g g-1), which not only demonstrates the potential applications of the mesoporous structure but also opens a new avenue to construct COF isomers and thus to increase the structural diversity of COFs and improve their applications.

document.output: Isomerism is a common phenomenon in organic chemistry but is not often seen in the covalent organic frameworks (COFs). Herein, we demonstrated the first example of controllable synthesis of COF topological isomers by using a unique tetrahedral OB unit precursor and diverse solvents, affording two 3D COFs with dia and qtz nets as isomers, denoted as JUC-620 and JUC-621. Their precise structures are confirmed by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) studies. Notably, the distinct porous structures are observed in these two isomers, in which the qtz-net COF JUC-621 shows permanent mesopores up to 23 Å in diameter and a specific surface area as high as 2060 m2 g-1, which is much larger than those of the microporous dia-net COF JUC-620 (pore size around 12 Å and specific surface area of 980 m2 g-1). Furthermore, the high surface area and mesoporous structure of JUC-621 endow it with good applicability for the removal of dye solutions and iodine adsorption with a capacity of up to 6.7 g g-1 (2.3 times that of JUC-620, 2.9 g g-1), which not only demonstrates the potential applications of the mesoporous structure but also opens a new avenue to construct COF isomers and thus to increase the structural diversity of COFs and improve their applications.
document.input: Although isomerism is a typical and significant phenomenon in organic chemistry, it is rarely found in covalent organic framework (COF) materials. Herein, for the first time, we report a controllable synthesis of topological isomers in three-dimensional COFs via a distinctive tetrahedral building unit under different solvents. Based on this strategy, both isomers with a dia or qtz net (termed JUC-620 and JUC-621) have been obtained, and their structures are determined by combining powder X-ray diffraction and transmission electron microscopy. Remarkably, these architectures show a distinct difference in their porous features; for example, JUC-621 with a qtz net exhibits permanent mesopores (up to ∼23 Å) and high surface area (∼2060 m2 g-1), which far surpasses those of JUC-620 with a dia net (pore size of ∼12 Å and surface area of 980 m2 g-1). Furthermore, mesoporous JUC-621 can remove dye molecules efficiently and achieves excellent iodine adsorption (up to 6.7 g g-1), which is 2.3 times that of microporous JUC-620 (∼2.9 g g-1).
